title: Central banks can't afford to keep missing their inflation targets
gist: The Iran war has pushed more major central banks off their inflation targets simultaneously than at any time since COVID-19, with US CPI at 4.2%; new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh is maintaining a hawkish 2% commitment but faces political pressure from Trump who nominated him to cut, while structurally sticky components (gasoline, fertilizer, food) mean price relief will lag any Hormuz peace agreement.

themes.theme: Synchronized global inflation overshoot driven by Iran war

insights.claim: More of the world's largest central banks are missing their inflation targets simultaneously than at any point since the height of COVID-19, driven by the Iran war's commodity shock via the Strait of Hormuz closure.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Synchronized global inflation overshoot constrains all major central banks from cutting simultaneously to support growth; watch for coordinated tightening that compresses global risk appetite.
insights.quote: More of the world's largest central banks are missing their targets today than at any point since the height of the COVID-19 shock.
insights.locator: Warsh isn't the only central bank leader

insights.claim: US CPI stands at 4.2%—the highest in over three years—with Australia, Brazil, and the Eurozone not far behind; the ECB is raising rates for the first time since 2023 to address eurozone inflation above its 2% target.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: The ECB's renewed rate hike cycle is a significant hawkish pivot with implications for European sovereign spreads, credit, and equity multiples—monitor ECB guidance closely.
insights.quote: The latest US consumer price index inflation rate sits at 4.2 percent—the highest in over three years—with Australia, Brazil, and the Eurozone not far behind.
insights.locator: The latest US consumer price index

insights.claim: South Korean inflation is at its highest in two years; Jordan was forced to pay an additional $200 million to import fuel in March (95% fuel-import dependent); Egypt raised energy prices by 31% in April—all attributed to the Iran war's commodity shock.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Emerging market commodity importers face dual pressure from higher import bills and social instability from subsidy costs; credit and currency risk is acute for countries like Jordan and Egypt.
insights.quote: South Korean inflation is at its highest in two years, and according to the finance ministry would have been even higher if not for nationwide fuel price caps
insights.locator: Brazil's inflation rate had sat inside the central bank's 1.5 to 4.5 percent band

themes.theme: Fed credibility and Warsh's political dilemma

insights.claim: New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh shortened the Fed's rate policy statement, eliminated forward guidance, and launched five task forces to study overhauls—a communication 'regime change' while maintaining a 'strong commitment' to the 2% inflation target.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Warsh's communication reset increases uncertainty for markets that relied on forward guidance to price rates; expect higher rate volatility and wider option skews on US rates.
insights.quote: he shortened the Fed's rate policy statement and opted to stop giving 'forward guidance'

insights.claim: Warsh's hawkish stance on the 2% inflation target will draw the ire of Trump, who nominated him expecting rate cuts—creating a structural tension between Fed independence and presidential pressure that is the key tail risk for US rates.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: If Trump pressures Warsh to cut into an above-target inflation environment, long-end US rates could spike and the dollar weaken sharply on credibility erosion.
insights.quote: this hawkishness and the potential for rate hikes later this year will draw the ire of the president who nominated him to do just the opposite
insights.locator: This puts Warsh in a delicate situation

insights.claim: US 5-10 year inflation expectations fell to 3.4% from 3.9% in May despite Strait of Hormuz closure—suggesting the public 'still half-believes that this most recent bout will pass'—but expectations remain well above the 2.8-3.2% range of 2024.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Long-run US inflation expectations are de-anchoring at a moderate pace; if they climb from 3.4% toward 4%+, the Fed faces a credibility crisis requiring aggressive rate hikes regardless of Trump pressure.
insights.quote: The University of Michigan's five-to-ten-year measure of those expectations dropped to 3.4 percent this month from 3.9 percent in May.
insights.locator: But despite the closure of the Strait of Hormuz

insights.claim: Even if a peace agreement with Iran reopens the Strait of Hormuz, price relief will not be immediate: gasoline 'rockets up but only slowly floats back down,' fertilizer shortages will spill into food prices, and supply chain uncertainty will keep overall prices elevated well past any ceasefire.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Opening Hormuz is not an immediate inflation cure—the reversal is asymmetric and slow; central banks will face a longer window of above-target inflation constraining their ability to ease.
insights.quote: Gasoline prices have a tendency to rocket up but only slowly float back down, fertilizer shortages will spill over into food prices, and lingering uncertainty means elevated prices will not dissipate immediately.
insights.locator: A peace agreement with Iran will provide cover
